title: Uses of cost information
summary: 9609 AS — how cost data supports pricing, profit analysis, cost control, and strategic decisions.
learningObjectives: Explain uses of cost data in business decisions.
Link cost information to pricing strategies.
Describe cost control and variance awareness.

simpleExplanation.title: Costs drive decisions
simpleExplanation.summary: Accurate costs support minimum pricing, show which products earn profit, highlight waste through budget comparison, and inform make-or-buy and closure decisions.
steps: Set floor price — must cover at least variable cost (contribution) short run.
Compare actual vs budget to control spending.

flashcards.front: How does cost data help pricing?
flashcards.back: Sets minimum price (cover VC or full cost depending on horizon); supports cost-plus and contribution-based pricing.
Compare actual costs to budget; investigate variances; hold managers accountable.
Identify which products/lines cover their costs and contribute most to profit.
Compare internal cost (including opportunity cost) with supplier quote — often using relevant costs only.
Past costs may not predict future — inflation, technology, and volume changes make data outdated.
Arbitrary overhead allocation can make a product look unprofitable when it is not.
Compare unit costs with industry best practice to find efficiency gaps.
Cost floor interacts with demand and positioning — price must cover costs AND match market willingness to pay.

faq.q: Should price always cover full unit cost?
faq.a: Long run: yes, or the business cannot survive. Short run: a promotional or special order may cover only variable costs if it contributes to fixed costs and does not displace better sales.

faq.q: How does this topic link to 5.4.4 break-even?
faq.a: Break-even is one specific use of contribution-based cost information — finding the output where total contribution equals fixed costs.

sections.content: **Uses of cost information** (5.4.3) connects **finance (5.4)** to **marketing pricing (3.3.4)** and **operations efficiency (4.1)**. Exam questions often ask you to **apply** cost data to a case decision — not recite definitions.

Cost data sets a **floor price**. In the **short run**, price must at least cover **variable costs** to earn contribution. In the **long run**, price must cover **full costs** plus profit.

**Cost-plus pricing** adds a markup to unit cost. **Contribution-based pricing** starts from market price and checks whether contribution is sufficient — common when demand is price-sensitive.
When evaluating pricing in a case, combine **cost floor** with **marketing** (elasticity, competition, brand). Cost alone does not set the optimal price.

Cost information shows **profit by product, department, or region**. Managers can drop unprofitable lines, promote high-contribution products, or renegotiate suppliers. **Customer profitability** analysis links revenue and cost to each segment.

Comparing **actual vs budget** costs highlights **variances** — favourable (spent less) or adverse (spent more). Investigation may reveal waste, inefficiency, or external price rises. Links forward to **5.5 budgets and variances**.

items: **Make-or-buy:** Compare relevant internal cost with external quote; ignore sunk costs.
**Close or continue:** If contribution is negative and no strategic reason to stay, consider closure.
**Capacity:** Rank uses of scarce resources by contribution per limiting factor.

sections.question:
A café sells coffee for $4. Variable cost per cup is $1.20. Fixed costs are $3 600/month. Budgeted sales 4 000 cups; actual sales 3 200 cups. Actual variable costs total $4 000.

Explain two uses of this cost information for the manager.
sections.solution:
**1. Pricing / break-even**
Contribution per cup = $4 − $1.20 = $2.80. Break-even = $3 600 ÷ $2.80 ≈ 1 286 cups. Manager knows minimum sales needed and can judge whether $4 price is viable.

**2. Cost control (variance)**
Budgeted VC for 3 200 cups = 3 200 × $1.20 = $3 840. Actual VC = $4 000 → **adverse variance of $160**. Manager should investigate (waste, supplier prices, portion sizes).

Also: actual sales below budget → lower total contribution; may need marketing or cost review.

sections.content: Cost information is **historical** and may not reflect future conditions. **Overhead allocation** can distort product profitability. **Non-financial factors** (quality, brand, staff morale) are excluded from cost accounts but matter in real decisions.
